items.q: What does natural law theory base morality on?
items.correct: Human nature and reason — there are objective purposes (telos) built into humans which reason can discover, generating universal moral precepts.
items.misconception: Natural law means whatever happens in nature is morally right.
items.random_wrong: Natural law is the same thing as the laws of physics.
items.why: The 'naturalistic fallacy' confusion — students assume natural law endorses lions eating gazelles or natural disasters. Aquinas's theory concerns human nature and rational ends, not all natural events.

items.q: What is the central principle of situation ethics?
items.correct: The only intrinsically good thing is agape love; rules can be broken if doing so produces the most loving outcome in the situation.
items.misconception: You should do whatever feels right in the moment.
items.random_wrong: Situation ethics says rules should always be followed.
items.why: Situation ethics is principled (love), not just intuition or relativism. Students collapse it into 'whatever feels right' — Fletcher's actual theory is more disciplined than that.

items.q: What does utilitarianism say is right action?
items.correct: The action that produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number — judged by consequences, not intentions or rules.
items.misconception: Whatever makes you personally happy is the right thing to do.
items.random_wrong: Utilitarianism says you should always tell the truth.
items.why: Students reduce utilitarianism to egoism. It is impartial — your own happiness counts no more than anyone else's. Personal happiness alone misses the aggregative core.

items.q: Are Fletcher's six propositions the same as his four working principles?
items.correct: No — the four working principles (pragmatism, relativism, positivism, personalism) are presuppositions of the method; the six propositions (love is the only intrinsic good, justice is love distributed, etc.) state the theory itself.
items.misconception: Fletcher's six propositions and four working principles are two names for one list.
items.random_wrong: Fletcher had ten commandments of situationism.
items.why: Mixing the lists is the most common factual slip in situation ethics answers. Four presuppositions frame the approach; six propositions unpack how love operates.

items.q: Does the doctrine of double effect allow a good intention to justify any means?
items.correct: No — among its conditions: the act itself must not be intrinsically wrong, and the good effect must not be produced by means of the bad effect; intention alone cannot launder a bad act.
items.misconception: Double effect says that as long as you intend something good, any action you take is justified.
items.random_wrong: Double effect says intentions are irrelevant to morality.
items.why: DDE is precisely a brake on 'the end justifies the means': the bad effect may only be a side-effect, never the chosen instrument. Students invert it into the consequentialism it was built to resist.

items.q: Which is Aquinas's primary precept ordered to community?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Living in society. “Self-preservation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The Doctrine of Double Effect requires that the bad effect be:
items.why: Correct answer: Unintended. “Greater than the good” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many fundamental propositions are in Fletcher's Situation Ethics?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
